picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL remains the highest-conviction name in the book: Google Cloud accelerated to +63% YoY in Q1 2026 (from +48% in Q4 2025), Search advertising is monetizing AI at scale, and the quality overlay is pristine — expanding FCF, improving ROIC, and accelerating EPS revisions. Berkshire tripled its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares (~$16.6B) in Q1 2026, the most pointed institutional endorsement in this 13F cycle, while Communication Services remains Tier 1 in sector rotation. The Warsh Fed hawkish pivot is a headwind to multiples broadly, but GOOGL's combination of accelerating Cloud growth + Search cash flows + buyback capacity gives it more fundamental cushion than most mega-caps at current valuations.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Google Cloud growth trajectory — whether +63% YoY accelerates or holds steady is the next major fundamental test
picks.entryNote: Trading at a discount to pure-play Tech peers on an earnings-growth-adjusted basis; FY2026 EPS consensus has been revised higher every week since Q1 print; consolidating above 50dma after Q1 earnings gap
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% for two consecutive quarters, or a structural loss of Search query share to AI-native competitors above 3 percentage points
picks.keyRisk: AI-native search alternatives (Perplexity, ChatGPT search) eroding Google's Search revenue per query, particularly in high-CPM commercial categories

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's custom ASIC and networking silicon franchise gives it differentiated AI infrastructure exposure complementary to — not duplicative of — NVDA's GPU dominance. Druckenmiller opened a new position in AVGO in Q1 2026, converging with existing smart money consensus. Susquehanna analyst Rolland flagged that 'Optical/AI Networking continues to be a bright spot as the 800G opportunity appears far larger than previously anticipated,' with shipments expected to nearly double in 2026 and again in 2027 — a direct AVGO tailwind. AVGO trades at a meaningful discount to NVDA on a forward P/E basis while serving the same hyperscaler customer base through a different supply chain layer.
picks.catalyst: Next earnings report (expected early June 2026) with AI revenue segment breakout and VMware integration margin update
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 25-27x forward EPS — a premium to the broader market but at a discount to NVDA; AI custom ASIC revenue growth inflecting; consolidating after post-Q4 move
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major custom ASIC design win at a hyperscaler, or AVGO gross margin falls below 64% on VMware integration dilution
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscalers bring ASIC design in-house (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium) and reduce reliance on AVGO custom silicon, compressing the AI revenue ramp

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA's Q1 FY2027 results delivered $82B revenue (+85% YoY), $1.87 EPS beating the $1.77 estimate, $49B free cash flow, and 74.9% gross margins — fundamentals that remain in lockstep with price momentum. Management guided Q2 revenue to $91B ±2%, and analysts note 'Nvidia is now seeing a trillion-dollar order book, at least through 2027.' The hyperscaler capex cycle remains intact: Google, Meta, and Amazon have all raised capex forecasts, with a combined $725B budgeted for full-year 2026. NVDA's next earnings are confirmed for August 26 — providing a clean 13-week window with no binary event risk and ongoing positive estimate revision momentum.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings on August 26, 2026 — confirmation of $91B revenue guidance and any update to the trillion-dollar order book
picks.entryNote: Stock at ~$213-222 range, ~$5.2T market cap; TTM total return ~99%; next earnings August 26 removes near-term binary risk; 61-analyst average 'Strong Buy' with $296 price target implying ~39% upside
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70% (from 74.9%), or a hyperscaler publicly reduces NVDA GPU orders citing capex ROI concerns
picks.keyRisk: Capex fatigue among hyperscalers — Google, AMZN, and META are financing data center builds with significant new debt, creating pressure on future orders if interest rates stay elevated

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AWS is in clear sequential re-acceleration: +20% → +24% → +28% YoY across the last three quarters, validating the cloud AI monetization thesis. Tepper nearly doubled his AMZN stake in Q1 2026 (98% increase, now 15.2% of Appaloosa's book) citing AWS expansion and 'buying a great stock at a discount after the February decline.' Ackman also added AMZN to his second-largest position. The combination of AWS margin expansion, accelerating ad revenue, and fulfillment efficiency gains produces one of the cleanest FCF/ROIC improvement trajectories in the universe. Q2 guidance from late April pointed to continued acceleration.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with AWS growth sustaining above 28% YoY and operating margin confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at a premium to historical P/E but FCF yield is expanding as AWS margins improve; Tepper's 98% stake increase at February levels provides a favorable cost basis reference; stock recovering from February correction lows
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 22% YoY, or retail segment operating margins reverse back below 5% due to tariff/fuel cost passthrough
picks.keyRisk: Energy cost inflation (Brent ~$120/barrel) compresses fulfillment and logistics margins in the retail segment while simultaneously raising AWS data center power costs

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman delivered a 7.4% Q1 2026 EPS beat with record Equities revenues, 24.3% EPS growth YoY, and a recovering IB pipeline as M&A activity re-opens. The sticky-rate environment (Fed on hold, real policy rate +90bp above neutral) is structurally favorable for GS's net interest income and trading revenues — higher-for-longer directly boosts Fixed Income and Equities volatility-driven trading P&L. Financials led positive revenue surprises in Q1 per FactSet, and Charles Schwab's Liz Ann Sonders added XLF to her 'growth sectors' list. GS is the highest-quality name in capital markets with a ROIC trajectory improving as the IB pipeline refills.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — IB fee acceleration as M&A pipeline clears, and any guidance on secondary market activity amid geopolitical volatility
picks.entryNote: Stock outperforming SPY on 3M, 6M, and 12M lookbacks; FY estimates revised upward post-Q1; trading at approximately 12-13x forward EPS vs. historical range of 10-14x — fair but not stretched for a cycle-high ROTCE environment
picks.exitTrigger: ROTCE falls below 17% for two consecutive quarters, or a material M&A market freeze driven by regulatory escalation under the new administration
picks.keyRisk: Fed policy uncertainty under Warsh — a surprise tightening cycle would invert the yield curve further and compress NII across capital markets, hitting trading revenue

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier expression of the geopolitical risk premium in the portfolio: the Iran conflict, extended U.S. naval blockade, and rising NATO defense budgets are multi-year structural tailwinds for Pratt & Whitney (engine MRO cycle) and Raytheon (missile systems, air defense). Defense backlogs stretch beyond 24 months, providing earnings visibility unusual for an industrial at this stage of the cycle. XLI total return of 29.87% over the past year confirms Industrials co-leadership, and defense names carry a specific geopolitical bid absent in most growth sectors. RTX also benefits from the commercial aerospace MRO cycle — a long-cycle recovery that has years of runway.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Pratt & Whitney GTF engine delivery ramp and Raytheon missile backlog conversion confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 20-21x forward EPS, in-line with Industrials sector average but with above-average earnings visibility from defense backlogs; 52-week momentum strongly positive vs. SPY
picks.exitTrigger: Iran conflict de-escalation leading to a rapid Hormuz reopening and reversal of the defense spending premium, or a Pratt & Whitney GTF engine recall event
picks.keyRisk: Geopolitical de-escalation — a rapid Iran ceasefire would remove the defense risk premium and potentially reprice RTX 10-15% lower quickly

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality expression of the energy trade: Brent near $120/barrel with the U.S. naval blockade of Iran extended indefinitely, the Energy sector recording the largest +8.6% increase in expected dollar-level earnings of all eleven sectors since December 31, and energy leading sector price performance YTD. XOM's disciplined capex and strong FCF profile at $120 Brent makes it a compounding cash flow machine — the stock trades at ~10x forward P/E, a deep discount to the market's 20.9x, providing asymmetric upside in an inflationary energy cycle. XLE was formally elevated to co-Tier 1 in the May 26-27 sector rotation update alongside XLK.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) realizing the full quarter of $120+ Brent crude in upstream margins, plus any update on Guyana and Permian production ramp
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~10x forward P/E vs. S&P 500 at 20.9x — a 50% discount to the market multiple; FCF yield well above 5% at current oil prices; technical base forming after YTD leadership move
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls below $85/barrel (Iran conflict resolution or OPEC+ production surge), compressing FCF yield to a level that makes the valuation discount less compelling
picks.keyRisk: Sudden Iran ceasefire and Hormuz reopening floods the market with ~20M barrels/day, causing a rapid $30-40/barrel oil price decline

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a high-quality compounder operating in an oligopolistic ratings and data analytics duopoly with pricing power independent of the economic cycle. In the current environment — high debt issuance (AI capex-driven bond supply from hyperscalers, $1.9T fiscal deficit funding demand), volatile credit markets, and rising M&A activity — SPGI's Ratings segment captures fees on every dollar issued and refinanced. The Financials sector has seen positive EPS revision breadth, and SPGI's subscription analytics segment (Market Intelligence, Platts) provides recurring revenue that is structurally uncorrelated to any single macro outcome. FCF yield is attractive and the ROIC is among the highest in the Financials sector.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Ratings revenue confirmation as AI capex bond issuance supply translates to fee revenue acceleration
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 28-30x forward EPS, a premium vs. market but justified by 20%+ ROIC and oligopoly pricing power; FY estimates trending higher on issuance activity; consolidating at technical support
picks.exitTrigger: Credit market seizure causing bond issuance volumes to collapse more than 30%, or regulatory action forcing the Ratings/analytics business separation
picks.keyRisk: A sovereign credit event (failed Treasury auction) or recession scare freezes corporate bond issuance and eliminates the Ratings revenue upside cycle

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK provides Healthcare sector diversification at a valuation that prices in excessive Keytruda patent cliff pessimism. Keytruda remains the world's best-selling oncology drug and the pipeline behind it — including the subQ formulation and next-generation co-formulations — provides a multi-year transition runway. At approximately 11-12x forward P/E with a 3%+ dividend yield, MRK trades near the low end of its 5-year range and well below the S&P 500 multiple, providing an asymmetric setup: limited downside if Keytruda revenue holds, meaningful upside if pipeline candidates (V116 vaccine, oral semaglutide competitor, oncology combinations) deliver. Healthcare sector adding a GICS diversification credit to the book.
picks.catalyst: Keytruda subQ FDA approval decision (expected H2 2026) and Q2 2026 earnings with pipeline update
picks.entryNote: Trading near 5-year P/E lows (~11-12x forward) vs. historical 14-16x average; 3%+ dividend yield provides income floor; de-rated to levels that price in significant Keytruda erosion not yet visible in revenue trends
picks.exitTrigger: Phase 3 failure of more than two next-generation Keytruda formulations in major oncology indications, or unexpected Keytruda safety label change
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda IRA price negotiation outcome (2026-2027 Medicare negotiation cycle) compresses U.S. pricing more than expected, materially impairing the 2028+ earnings bridge

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the highest-conviction way to play AI data center power demand within the Utilities sector without accepting the 3-7 year regulatory lag that constrains regulated peers. Vistra's merchant generation fleet (natural gas + nuclear) can reprice to spot power markets in real-time, capturing the full electricity demand shock from hyperscaler data center load growth. Tepper doubled his VST position in Q1 2026, citing power demand from AI data centers. The Energy and Utilities co-sector momentum (XLE/XLU benefiting from defense and power-demand themes) supports continued relative outperformance, and VST's nuclear fleet provides a clean energy premium for data center offtake contracts.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) with power price realization in ERCOT and data center offtake contract announcements
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 15-17x forward EPS — a premium to regulated utilities but a discount to technology names with similar growth profiles; nuclear fleet provides clean energy contract premium; momentum strongly positive vs. SPY YTD
picks.exitTrigger: ERCOT power prices collapse below $40/MWh for a sustained period, or a major hyperscaler announces its data center power sourcing strategy shifts away from merchant contracts to self-generation
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory intervention on power market pricing in ERCOT, or energy price reversal (Brent/natural gas) compressing VST's merchant generation margins

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold at $4,484 is the direct beneficiary of three simultaneously elevated risks: sovereign fiscal stress (U.S. deficit at $1.9T/5.8% of GDP, debt-to-GDP at 101% and rising), geopolitical escalation (Iran war/Hormuz closure, extended U.S. naval blockade), and a Fed that is paralyzed — unable to ease (inflation re-accelerating toward 4.18% CPI per Cleveland Fed) yet unable to credibly tighten given debt service costs. GLD has near-zero correlation to equities in genuine risk-off events and provides the portfolio's primary hedge against the sovereign credibility break tail risk. Persistently high fiscal deficits and rising debt-to-GDP represent the dominant structural bid.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June release) confirming 4%+ trajectory, or any Hormuz disruption escalation removing another leg of oil supply
picks.entryNote: Gold recently trading at $4,484 — near all-time highs but with fundamental support from deficit, geopolitical, and inflation drivers all simultaneously present; momentum and accumulation signals remain positive
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire and rapid Hormuz reopening combined with a meaningful Fed rate cut path materializing (2+ cuts confirmed), reducing both geopolitical and fiscal fear premiums simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A sharp USD strengthening event (safe-haven dollar bid in a global risk-off) temporarily suppresses gold prices despite the underlying inflationary/fiscal thesis remaining intact

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD's dividend quality screen naturally excludes AI-bubble names with no FCF, overweighting Financials, Healthcare, Industrials, and Consumer Staples — sectors with low VIX correlation and demonstrated earnings stability. The ETF yields ~3.5%+, providing an income buffer in drawdowns, and its factor tilts (value + quality + dividend growth) historically outperform in late-cycle environments where multiple compression pressures pure-growth names. In 2025, equity gains broadened beyond mega-cap tech to cyclical sectors such as industrials and financials — SCHD's composition captures this broadening while maintaining defensive characteristics.
picks.catalyst: Ongoing dividend income compounding and potential outperformance in a risk-off or multiple-compression environment driven by hawkish Fed signals
picks.entryNote: Trading at a meaningful discount on a P/E basis to the S&P 500 given its value/quality tilt; ~3.5% yield provides income while awaiting capital appreciation; low correlation to high-multiple tech names in the growth book
picks.exitTrigger: A broad cyclical earnings collapse (recession scenario) that impairs the dividend capacity of the underlying portfolio companies across multiple sectors simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A 'growth scare' recession scenario where Financials, Industrials, and Consumer Staples all cut dividends simultaneously — SCHD's diversification partially mitigates but cannot eliminate this tail

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire's $397B cash war chest (larger than its entire $263B equity book) is the most explicit macro-caution signal from institutional investors in this cycle — it earns ~5%+ in T-bills while waiting to deploy at distress prices, providing an embedded carry floor. The restored buyback program under Greg Abel adds a shareholder return mechanism. BRK.B's diversified exposure across insurance, energy, rails, and consumer requires no single thesis to work, and its low-teens P/E with no AI multiple and no leverage makes it a natural diversifier against the concentrated tech exposure in the growth book.
picks.catalyst: Any market dislocation creating a Berkshire deployment opportunity (fiscal shock, credit event) that Abel uses the $397B war chest to capitalize on
picks.catalystWindow: Evergreen — opportunistic; next quarterly update August 2026
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 1.5-1.6x book value and low-to-mid teens P/E — below the S&P 500 forward multiple; $397B cash provides a hard floor under intrinsic value; Abel's GOOGL tripling signals willingness to deploy selectively
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes multiple large acquisitions that significantly reduce the cash fortress below $200B without a corresponding market dislocation justifying deployment
picks.keyRisk: A prolonged bull market in which Berkshire's cash position is a perpetual drag on relative performance vs. a fully-invested benchmark, creating sustained underperformance

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF at a 10-year yield of 4.57% offers genuine carry (4.4%+ distribution yield) combined with flight-to-quality convexity in a genuine risk-off shock. The intermediate 7-10 year duration is superior to TLT in the current environment given elevated fiscal deficit risk at the long end — BofA forecasts the 10-year ending 2026 at 4.00-4.25%, implying ~30-50bp of potential price appreciation from here if the growth scare scenario materializes. IEF provides the portfolio's most direct hedge against an AI capex disappointment or consumer recession scenario that forces the Fed to eventually reverse course.
picks.catalyst: Thursday May 28 April PCE print (BofA forecasts 0.3% m/m core, 3.3% y/y) — if it prints below consensus it re-opens easing expectations and provides an immediate IEF price catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Tomorrow (May 28) PCE print, then June 17 FOMC press conference
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.57% is at the upper end of the post-2022 range; an intermediate-term duration focus in investment-grade bonds is preferred per current risk assessments; modestly sized given rate-hike tail risk from hawkish FOMC
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks above 5.25% on a sustained basis (fiscal shock / failed auction scenario) forcing duration reduction, or Fed confirms a rate-hike cycle that pushes front-end above 4.5%
picks.keyRisk: The fiscal shock tail risk — if Moody's triggers a bond vigilante moment and 10-year yields spike through 5.5%, IEF suffers a meaningful mark-to-market loss despite its intermediate duration positioning

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the canonical late-cycle/stagflationary defensive: global brand dominance with genuine pricing power (demonstrably passed through in 2022-2024 inflation cycle), 62+ consecutive years of dividend growth, and a balance sheet/free cash flow profile that has never failed to sustain the dividend. In a hawkish-Fed, sticky-inflation, softening-labor-market environment, KO's ~3% dividend yield and low earnings volatility provide ballast against the more aggressive growth positions. Berkshire's KO position (11.56% of the portfolio, 3rd-largest holding) provides institutional endorsement. Consumer Staples fulfill the defensive sleeve's required diversification into the consumer staples exposure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — confirmation that organic revenue growth sustains above 4% despite volume pressure from lower-income consumer fatigue
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 22x forward EPS — the sector multiple is 22x vs. 4-5% EPS growth, which is rich in isolation, but KO's pricing power and dividend growth history justify a premium to staples peers; Berkshire's continued 11.56% portfolio allocation provides a price floor signal
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth decelerates below 2% for two consecutive quarters (indicating genuine pricing power exhaustion), or a volume decline of more than 5% in the critical North America and EMEA markets
picks.keyRisk: Consumer trade-down accelerates past Coca-Cola into private-label beverages at a pace that permanently impairs the premium pricing and volume metrics of the core sparkling portfolio

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle, stagflationary overlay regime where nominal earnings growth remains strong (+27.7% S&P 500 blended Q1 YoY) but real policy is restrictive (+90bp above neutral), the Fed under Warsh faces asymmetric pressure toward tightening not easing, and geopolitical risk (Iran/Hormuz closure, Brent ~$120) has permanently re-priced energy and defense. The 10-name growth book expresses four high-conviction themes simultaneously: AI infrastructure value chain (GOOGL, AVGO, NVDA, AMZN), geopolitical/energy beneficiaries (XOM, RTX, VST), financial-cycle alpha (GS, SPGI), and defensive value with Healthcare diversification (MRK) — spanning 6 GICS sectors while maintaining the thesis continuity required for a concentrated book. All prior-week carry-overs (GOOGL #1, AVGO #2, NVDA #3, AMZN #4, GS #5, RTX #6, XOM #7, SPGI #8, VST #10) are maintained because no thesis-breaking event has occurred; MRK replaces LLY at #9 (LLY de-ranked given NVO/GLP-1 competitive dynamics and LLY's richer valuation relative to MRK's deeper discount). The dominant risk being underwritten is the hawkish Fed policy pivot under Warsh materializing faster than equity multiples have adjusted, with the Cleveland Fed's Q2-annualized 6.85% inflation rate creating a plausible scenario where the next Fed move is a hike, not a cut — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is sized at 27% to provide meaningful ballast against that scenario.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX), Energy (XOM), Utilities (VST), and Health Care (MRK) — 7 distinct GICS sectors against the 5-sector minimum, with the accepted factor concentration being two AI-infrastructure names (NVDA, AVGO) sharing secular exposure but operating at different supply chain layers (GPU compute vs. custom ASIC/networking). The defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), diversified holding company/cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — covering all four required defensive exposure categories with no duplication.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL delivered a 93.6% Q1 EPS beat at $5.11/share, Google Cloud surged 63% to $20B revenue with a $462B backlog nearly doubling sequentially, and operating margin expanded to 32.9%. Berkshire tripled its stake to ~$16.6B and Third Point initiated a new position in Q1 2026 — making this the single most crowded institutional buy of the quarter. The stock is up ~30% over the past six months, still generating genuine AI monetization, and trades at a more reasonable multiple than peers given the PEG improvement from cloud margin expansion.
picks.catalyst: Google Cloud backlog conversion into revenue and continued AI Overviews/Search monetization in Q2 2026 earnings (late July)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~27-28x forward EPS — rich vs. 10-year average but justified by cloud margin tripling to 32.9% from 17.8% YoY; consolidating above the 50dma after +30% 6-month run
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or antitrust breakup remedies materially impair Search monetization
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies forcing structural separation of Search and/or Android distribution

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom posted a 9.04% Q1 EPS beat at $2.05 vs $1.88 estimated, with a PEG ratio of just 0.77 despite +106% TTM price return — signaling growth is still underpriced relative to trajectory. ROIC of 21.33% and ROE of 33.37% confirm capital efficiency. Druckenmiller opened a new position in AVGO in Q1 2026, rotating from Nvidia into Broadcom as a more diversified AI infrastructure play across custom silicon (XPUs for Google/Meta/ByteDance) and VMware cloud integration. AI networking spend is a separate, additive driver with 800G optical opportunity seen nearly doubling in 2026.
picks.catalyst: June 3 fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report — custom AI ASIC revenue update and VMware integration synergies
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~31.9x forward P/E vs. historical range of 18-35x; near-term consolidation after +106% TTM run creates a reasonable entry ahead of a high-visibility earnings catalyst
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of custom XPU design wins at Google or Meta, or VMware customer churn above 15%
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — top 3 hyperscaler XPU customers represent majority of AI revenue; any single cancellation is a material earnings event

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA just reported Q1 FY27 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with $49B free cash flow and 74.9% gross margins, and guided Q2 to $91B vs. $86.84B consensus — a clean upside beat with forward momentum intact. Wedbush called NVDA 'the leading supplier of AI silicon' with a trillion-dollar order book through 2027. The AI infrastructure supercycle nowcast of +11.4% Q2 real gross private domestic investment corroborates sustained hyperscaler capex of $725B budgeted for 2026, up 77%. Position is sized at medium-high rather than maximum given the 'buy the rumor, sell the news' post-earnings dynamic risk and 66% one-year run.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY27 guidance of $91B converts to reported revenue — next print in August 2026, plus Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading around $135-145 range post-May 20 print; stock has historically seen post-earnings chop before resuming trend — current entry is into the post-report consolidation window
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70% or Q3 guidance below $90B, signaling Blackwell supply constraints exceeding demand
picks.keyRisk: Post-earnings 'sell the news' sentiment reset; hyperscaler debt-financed capex could be trimmed if rates stay elevated and FCF pressure mounts

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a high-conviction defense compounder with continuous contract flow — a $441.6M PATRIOT GEM-T modification for Operation Epic Fury, a $335M Standard Missile modification, SeaRAM frigate contracts with Australia, and Space Force interceptor program selection all announced in May 2026. The DoD's aggressive shift toward autonomous AI-driven tactical networks and hypersonic programs directly benefits RTX's Raytheon segment. The 12-month analyst consensus target of $215.27 implies 22.3% upside from current levels, with a 23-analyst Buy consensus and a 7.4% dividend increase just declared. Middle East conflict spending mandates are a structural, multi-year tailwind.
picks.catalyst: CEO Chris Calio speaking at Bernstein Strategic Decisions Conference May 29 — expected to provide backlog and margin guidance update
picks.catalystWindow: May 29, 2026 (next 7 days) then Q2 earnings July
picks.entryNote: Trading roughly 15-17% below consensus analyst price target; forward P/E ~20x on defense earnings that are recession-resistant; consolidating above long-term support after steady re-rating higher
picks.exitTrigger: Middle East ceasefire reducing defense procurement urgency, or GTF engine liability charges materially exceeding $7B reserve
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal engine liability remains an open-ended balance sheet risk that could resurface with incremental charges

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Financials are the #2 ranked sector in the rotation screen, with Goldman specifically positioned as the premier capital markets beneficiary of deal flow reactivation and a steeper yield curve. Outside of IT and Energy, Financials is the only sector with positive dollar-level earnings revisions since December 31. Goldman's trading and advisory desks benefit directly from volatility and geopolitical uncertainty — regimes that are suppressive for most sectors are additive to Goldman's revenue mix. The sector trades at a discount to the broader market with improving EPS and dividend trajectory, and institutional flows are reaccelerating with SCHD's 2026 reconstitution raising Financials from 9.7% to 15%+.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (mid-July) — trading revenue and M&A advisory pipeline against backdrop of reactivating deal markets
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~10-11x forward earnings — meaningful discount to SPX at ~20x; yield curve steepening (2s10s turning positive) directly expands NII for banking conglomerates; technically in an uptrend
picks.exitTrigger: Credit cycle turns with HY OAS widening above 450bps, or capital markets deal flow stalls in a recessionary environment
picks.keyRisk: Late-cycle credit deterioration — Goldman's trading book has tail risk to a rapid HY spread blow-out if recession materializes faster than consensus expects

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is trading at ~$1,044 today (May 22) after its next-generation retatrutide weight-loss drug cleared a late-stage Phase 3 clinical trial — a direct pipeline catalyst adding to the already-dominant GLP-1 oligopoly with Mounjaro and Zepbound. The company has committed ~$23B in manufacturing capex to expand fill-finish capacity through 2027, removing the supply ceiling that capped 2025 revenue. Unlike NVO, which faces semaglutide patent cliffs and MFN pricing pressure, LLY benefits from NVO's headwinds by capturing market share. Druckenmiller increased his LLY stake to 100,675 shares in Q1 2026, validating the quality compounder thesis.
picks.catalyst: Retatrutide Phase 3 full data readout and potential FDA filing timeline — plus Q2 earnings in late July with Zepbound volume trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,044, well below 52-week high of $1,133; ~30-35x forward earnings is reasonable for a 20%+ earnings growth compounder with secular obesity + Alzheimer's pipeline; bouncing off multi-month support
picks.exitTrigger: MFN pricing executive order expands to commercial markets, or oral GLP-1 competitor (e.g., Pfizer danuglipron) achieves superior efficacy data in Phase 3
picks.keyRisk: Medicare/Medicaid GLP-1 coverage rollback under 'One Big Beautiful Bill' reduces total addressable market and realized pricing

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a capital-light, high-ROIC compounder with structural revenue tied to debt issuance, index licensing, and data analytics — all tailwinds in a late-cycle environment where corporate debt issuance remains robust and passive index AUM continues compounding. HY OAS at 276bps (near multi-year tights) and IG OAS at ~80bps reflect a functioning credit market still generating ratings and issuance fees. SPGI's Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights segments diversify revenue beyond pure credit cycles. The business has pricing power embedded in long-term contracts and a near-monopoly in index licensing alongside MSCI.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — debt issuance volumes in Q2 driven by corporate refinancing activity and sovereign bond supply will directly confirm revenue trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~28-30x forward earnings — below its 5-year average premium of 33x; credit market stability at current spread levels supports a stable issuance fee baseline going into Q2
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens sharply above 400bps (credit stress kills issuance), or regulatory challenge to ratings oligopoly business model

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing as of Friday, May 22, 2026.

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — May 22, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**

The FOMC held the target range for the fed funds rate at **3.50%–3.75%** at its March 18, 2026 meeting.
 
The March 2026 dot plot median signals only **25 bps of cuts in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027**, with the Fed projecting PCE inflation to end 2026 at **2.7%** — 30 bps higher than its December 2025 projection.
 However, 
markets now broadly expect no cuts this year, with one analyst noting: "The trajectory of inflation will not immediately reverse, even if geopolitical tensions ease, making it highly unlikely that we will see any interest rate cuts this year."
 The Fed's longer-run neutral rate is estimated around 3.0%, implying the real policy rate (~+0.8% above 2.7% projected inflation) remains only modestly restrictive. 
Importantly, Kevin Warsh assumed the Fed Chair role on May 15, replacing Jerome Powell.


**YIELD CURVE**

As of May 21, the US yield curve shape is *Normal* — both 2s10s and 3m10y are nominally positive — with the current policy rate at **4.25%** (upper bound).
 
The 10-year yield sits roughly around **4.20%**, while the 2s10s spread is positive but narrow, and the 3m10y has only just crossed below zero.
 
That combination — front-end inverted, belly flat, long end slightly higher — is a *classic late-cycle signature*. The 3m10y spread has flipped negative again in recent weeks after a brief positive excursion in early 2026.
 The 30-year was at **~4.88%** as of mid-March. Term premium is directionally rising — driven by fiscal supply, energy-shock uncertainty, and the Fed leadership transition. 
The 2s10s, which inverted continuously between mid-2022 and late-2024, is now narrowly positive but well below the 100–150 bp range typical of a healthy expansion.


**INFLATION**

April CPI: Core CPI rose **+0.4% m/m**, with inflation ticking up 0.2 ppt to **2.8% y/y**.
 
Shelter costs rose 0.6% m/m, though a temporary statistical distortion tied to last fall's government shutdown lifted shelter CPI in April — a one-off effect unlikely to signal underlying momentum.
 
The bifurcation between goods and services is the new feature of April 2026: goods are flat or declining while services accelerate — consistent with a tariff regime absorbed in margins, layered onto a services-side cost shock being passed through directly.
 
Core PCE stood at **3.0% YoY** through February
; 
April core PCE (due May 30) will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, with consensus expecting roughly **0.25–0.35% m/m**, pushing YoY toward **2.9–3.1%**.
 
Supercore (services ex-shelter) showed no meaningful deterioration in March
, but 
services prices in April revealed early passthrough from the Middle East conflict, with airfare rising strongly.
 Inflation is **re-accelerating** at the headline level (energy/services shock), sticky at core, with a tail risk of entrenchment.

**GROWTH**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is **+4.3%** as of May 21, up from 4.0% on May 14.
 
Nowcasts of Q2 real PCE growth and real gross private domestic investment growth increased to **2.9%** and **11.4%**, respectively.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 2026 real GDP growth was **+2.0%**, 0.8 ppt above the final GDPNow nowcast.
 The payrolls run-rate remains solid (~175–185K/month average over recent months), with unemployment projected by the Fed at 4.4% in Q4 2026. ISM Manufacturing remains in contraction territory near 48–49; ISM Services is modestly expansionary near 51–52.

**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~2.76%** (276 bps) in May 2026
 — near multi-year tights and not signaling stress. 
Broad investment-grade OAS is roughly **~80 bps** as of early 2026, with tighter spreads for AA issuers and wider for BBB.
 Financial conditions remain broadly accommodative. 
The DXY (USD index) closed at approximately **99.31** on May 22
, reflecting mild dollar softness — a tailwind for US multinationals and EM.

**EARNINGS**

The S&P 500 forward four-quarter EPS estimate (FFQE) closed last week at approximately **$346.82**, with the earnings yield at **4.68%** as of May 15.
 
Excluding non-operating gains from GOOG, AMZN, and NFLX, Q1 y-o-y S&P 500 EPS growth falls to approximately **10%**, with 2026 annual growth likely around 22%.
 
The trailing S&P 500 earnings yield is **3.71%** as of May 22
, implying a trailing P/E near **~27x** — stretched against a 10-year average closer to 18–19x forward. 
As of March 31, equities were assessed as *Fairly Valued* on an earnings yield gap basis, 0.30 standard deviations below historical average.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**This is a late-cycle regime with a geopolitical overlay.** 
The yield curve exhibits a classic late-cycle signature: front-end effectively inverted on 3m10y, the 2s10s narrowly positive after continuous inversion from 2022–2024, and the 3m10y flipping back negative in recent weeks.
 The policy rate at 3.50–3.75% remains above neutral (~3.0%), labor markets are still tight but moderating (Fed projects 4.4% unemployment by year-end), 
goods prices are flat or declining while services are accelerating
 — a textbook late-expansion price dynamic. 
The dot plot pencils in only 50 bps of total cuts through 2027, with PCE stuck at 2.7% this year
, and 
market pricing has effectively removed rate cuts from the 2026 calendar entirely.
 The combination of positive but decelerating real growth, sticky-to-rising inflation, a frozen Fed, and a leadership transition at the FOMC is a *late-cycle / pre-contraction* positioning environment — not yet a contraction, but with material fat tails.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

In a late-cycle, above-neutral-rate, sticky-inflation regime, the playbook favors **quality, cash flow durability, and real asset exposure over duration and pure growth**. What's **working**: (1) **Large-cap Energy and Defense** — 
gas prices are up ~28.4% YoY and averaging $4.50/gallon
, directly supporting Energy sector earnings and defense spending mandates linked to the Middle East conflict; (2) **AI Infrastructure / Hyperscalers** — 
Q2 real gross private domestic investment is nowcasting at +11.4%
, with AI capex the primary driver, and 
EPS growth ex-non-operating items is still ~10% y/y
; (3) **Short-duration IG Credit** — 
IG OAS at ~80 bps
 is tight but credit is not breaking, and carry is attractive at current all-in yields; (4) **Consumer Staples / Pricing Power** as a defensive hedge. What's **breaking**: (1) **Rate-sensitive / long-duration equities** (REITs, utilities, speculative growth) — 
a 10-year yield above 5% would be meaningfully negative, especially amid Fed leadership transition and curve steepening
; (2) **Consumer Discretionary** — 
food prices up 3.2% YoY and beef up 14.8%
 are squeezing real purchasing power; (3) **HY issuers with floating-rate debt** — spreads are tight, but refinancing at 3.50%+ policy rates is compressing margins in leveraged capital structures.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (2–3 Highest-Impact Events, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **April Core PCE (May 30)** — 
The Fed's preferred gauge; PCE weights shelter less heavily than CPI, so April core PCE will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, but consensus expects YoY ticking toward **2.9–3.1%** from March's 3.0%.
 A print above 3.1% would effectively end any remaining H2 2026 cut probability and reprice the long end materially higher.

2. **Iran/Middle East Conflict Resolution — Energy Price Trajectory** — 
Economists note inflationary effects of the war could take weeks to months to unwind, with an optimistic normalization timeline of ~2 months if resolved soon, or six to nine months in a pessimistic scenario.
 
Lingering geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are likely to add further upward pressure through higher fuel costs, feeding into transportation prices in the coming months.
 An escalation or a durable ceasefire is the single highest-variance input to the inflation trajectory and Fed optionality.

3. **New Fed Chair Warsh's First Communications / FOMC Policy Signals** — 
Warsh assumed the Chair role on May 15; his first communications will be parsed carefully for whether he frames April's inflation data as an energy outlier (dovish read) or a services breakout (hawkish read).
 Any pivot in the Fed's reaction function under new leadership — particularly on the inflation tolerance threshold — is the key institutional risk for the rate/equity complex over the next 1–6 months. Watch the June FOMC (June 17–18) and the updated SEP dot plot as the definitive signal.

---
*All data as of market close May 22, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical, not investment advice.*

## 3. 🟠 MACRO TAIL RISKS (12-Month Horizon)

### RISK #1: U.S. Fiscal Shock / "Sovereign Credibility" Break
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**

**What would trigger it:** 
The deficit totals $1.9 trillion in fiscal year 2026 and is projected to grow to $3.1 trillion by 2036, representing 5.8% of GDP in 2026 — nearly double the 50-year average of 3.8%.
 
Debt held by the public is on track to rise from 101% of GDP in 2026 to 120% in 2036, well above the previous record set just after World War II.
 The trigger is a failed Treasury auction, an additional sovereign downgrade (following Moody's 2025 action), or a bond vigilante moment where 10-year yields spike through 5.5% on deficit/supply concerns.

**What gets hurt most:** TLT (long duration bonds collapse), rate-sensitive growth stocks (high-P/E tech, utilities like NEE, REITs like AMT/EQIX/PLD), leveraged buyout pipeline (BLK, KKR ecosystem). 
The IMF explicitly flagged that the U.S. fiscal trajectory creates a growing financial stability tail risk for the global economy given the centrality of the Treasury market.

**Probability Tier: MEDIUM (Rising)**

**What would trigger it:** 
The AI boom is already a much larger story for markets than for the economy so far.
 
As valuations have risen, there is more vulnerability to disappointment about the pace of innovation or monetization, or to shifts in the technology that reduce the capital or energy intensity of AI model training.
 The trigger: a hyperscaler (META, MSFT, GOOGL, AMZN) cuts capex guidance citing unclear AI ROI, or a major enterprise customer publicly abandons an AI deployment.


The market is already pricing AI capital spending against evidence of returns, not just on the size of the commitment — a meaningful new differentiation from prior years.

### RISK #3: Stagflationary Re-Acceleration (Tariff + Sticky Services Inflation)
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**

**What would trigger it:** 
Inflation moved sideways during 2025 as tariffs boosted goods prices while services inflation moderated.
 
An unresolved issue remains around the inflationary impact of U.S. tariffs. If tariffs raise the baseline level of inflation, it could complicate the Fed's ability to cut rates before reaching its 2% target, highlighting ongoing tension between fiscal and monetary policy.

The U.S. economy of 2026 is not the economy of 1981 — federal debt held by the public stands at approximately 100% of GDP, compared to roughly 25% when Volcker began his tightening cycle.
 
Corporate debt levels and household debt service costs are similarly elevated, meaning the policy tools available to combat stagflation are narrower than they were, and the cost of using them aggressively would be higher.
